attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic with a six-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She reports breakthrough bleeding despite combined oral contraceptive pills and tranexamic acid. She finds tampons ineffective and has not previously had cervical screening.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Speculum examination reveals a longitudinal vaginal septum and two separate cervices. Transvaginal ultrasound shows complete duplication of the uterine cavity with two widely separated uterine horns, each with its own cervix.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of uterine didelphys, which occurs due to complete failure of fusion of the Müllerian ducts during embryological development. The key diagnostic features are the presence of two completely separate uterine cavities, each with its own cervix, and a longitudinal vaginal septum.

Uterine didelphys is characterized by complete duplication of the reproductive tract, including two separate uterine bodies, two cervices, and often a longitudinal vaginal septum. This matches the findings on both physical examination and ultrasound in this case.

Septate uterus occurs when the Müllerian ducts fuse but the septum fails to resorb. This results in a single uterus with normal external contour but divided internally by a septum. There is typically a single cervix.

Unicornuate uterus results from the incomplete development of one Müllerian duct, leading to a single-horned uterus with a single cervix. This is clearly not consistent with the findings of two separate uterine horns.

Arcuate uterus is considered a normal variant with a mild indentation at the uterine fundus. It has a single cavity and cervix, making it inconsistent with this patient's presentation.

Bicornuate uterus shows partial fusion of the Müllerian ducts, resulting in two separate uterine horns but with a single cervix. The uterine horns are typically closer together than in uterine didelphys, and there is no vaginal septum.

Women with uterine didelphys may be asymptomatic or present with dysmenorrhoea or irregular bleeding. They are at increased risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm delivery and malpresentation. Associated renal anomalies should be investigated as the urinary and reproductive systems develop in close association.
learningPoints: Uterine didelphys is characterized by complete duplication of the uterus, cervix, and vagina due to failed fusion of the Müllerian ducts.
The presence of a longitudinal vaginal septum and double cervix on examination are pathognomonic features of uterine didelphys.
Transvaginal ultrasound showing two widely separated uterine horns is diagnostic of uterine didelphys.
references: Akhtar MA, Saravelos SH, Li TC, Jayaprakasan K; on behalf of the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Reproductive implications and management of congenital uterine anomalies. Scientific Impact Paper No. 62. BJOG. 2019 Nov 20; DOI: 10.1111/1471-0528.15968.

Crowley CM, Botros K, Hegazy IF, O'Donnell E. Uterine didelphys: diagnosis, management and pregnancy outcome. BMJ Case Rep. 2021 Mar 29;14(3):e242233. doi: 10.1136/bcr-2021-242233. PMID: 33782076; PMCID: PMC8009243.
